original_abstract: We present a keyword-based query interface for knowledge bases—including relational and deductive databases—that leverages contextual background knowledge such as join conditions and synonyms to interpret user intent. In our framework, join paths can be identified via existing foreign key constraints or, in cases where schema constraints are absent, learned from historical query patterns. By transforming a simple list of keywords into a structured logical term, our approach bridges the gap between natural language and formal database queries. The core of this system is an intelligent, cooperative parsing mechanism that hypothesizes the underlying semantics of a word list and reconstructs missing links between concepts using database metadata. We implement this via an extended version of Definite Clause Grammars (DCG) that dynamically interrogates the database schema to annotate tokens as table names, attributes, values, or relationships. Our tool, DDQL, translates these keyword inputs into a set of candidate queries expressed in an extended Datalog-based domain-specific language, allowing users to select the interpretation that best matches their information needs.
humanized_abstract: We present a keyword-based query interface to the knowledge in knowledge bases (KBs) like relational and deductive databases. The key idea here is to make use of contextual background knowledge such as the join conditions and the synonyms of the tables, attributes and values in the KB. We can compute join paths in the KB based on foreign key constraints between the tables in the KB schema. In absence of any schema constraints for tables in the KB schema, the join paths can also be computed based on query patterns from the set of queries that have been issued to the KB in the past. Our method translates a simple list of keywords into a logical expression that acts as a bridge between natural language and the formal database queries. It makes use of an intelligent and cooperative parsing method that not only guesses the intended semantics for a given word list but also recovers the connections between the words based on the knowledge provided by the KB. The KB is exploited by using an extension to the Definite Clause Grammars (DCG) parsing paradigm, where the database schema is “queryed” on the fly to infer annotations to each token that appears in the word list such as a table name, an attribute name, a value, or an association between two tokens in the word list. The KB interface tool, referred to as DDQL, takes as input the keywords of a user query and produces as output a number of formal database queries — expressed in terms of an extension of Datalog — which correspond to possible parses for the user query that the system can select according to its intended meaning.
